15 min read
“Tactics without strategy is the noise before defeat.” — Sun Tzu
Edward Luttwak has been called The Machiavelli of Maryland.
Unless you’re a president or prime minister, you’ve probably never
heard of him. (If you have, we’re impressed.)
People in power contact Edward Luttwak with unusual requests.
The prime minister of Kazakhstan wanted to �nd a way to
remove ethnic Russians from a city on his northern border…
A major Asian government wanted a plan to train its new
intelligence services…
An Italian chemical company wanted help settling an asbestos
lawsuit with a local commune…
A citizens’ group in Tonga wanted to scare away Japanese dolphin
poachers from its shores…
The president of Mexico sought Luttwak’s insights to help
eliminate a street gang that was burning tourist buses in the city
of Mexicali…
Strategy + 10,000′ 	View

-- 1 of 22 --

European governments have hired him to root out al-Qaida
operatives, and the US army has commissioned him to update its
counterinsurgency manual.
Even the Dalai Lama has consulted him about relations with
China…
Edward Luttwak at his home in Maryland.
Edward Luttwak earns upwards of a million dollars a year as a
self-proclaimed “grand strategist.”
He describes what he does as the “logic of strategy,” which is
informed by three counter-intuitive ideas:
▪ Inef�ciency
▪ Unpredictability

-- 2 of 22 --

▪ Mastery of emotion
Inef�ciency:
“…’the logic of strategy’…usually involves doing the least e�cient thing
possible in order to gain the upper hand over your enemy by confusing
them. If your tank battalion has the choice of a good highway or a bad
road, take the bad road…”
Unpredictability:
“As a strategist, Luttwak sees the presumption of predictability as a
damning vulnerability.”
Mastery of emotions:
“…most people cannot master their emotions. Above all, strategy is
about mastering your emotions.”
The Art of Email is informed by Edward Luttwak’s logic of
strategy. We’re not �ghting enemies on a battle�eld or dealing
with complex geo-political problems, but we still can bene�t from
a strategic decision-making framework that informs our choices.
Let’s start with inef�ciency.
The fastest, most ef�cient way to acquire a customer is to send all
of your traf�c to an offer immediately. This approach is the
foundation of most direct-response marketing.
Ad > Sales Page > Upsell(s)

-- 3 of 22 --

We see the same approach with conventional lead generation.
Ad > Landing Page with ‘Ethical Bribe’ that Requires an Opt-In >
Promotional Email Sequence > Sales Page > Upsell(s)
On the surface, this methodology seems to make sense because
it’s highly ef�cient. Each step leads clearly and invariably to the
next.
However, as we learned in the AoE Manifesto, only a small
minority of customers will convert immediately. That means
optimizing for short-term ef�ciency is actually undermining our
long-term pro�tability.
That’s a bad trade.
AoE is purposefully inef�cient in the short term to maximize
results in the long term. We don’t need to rush to a sale that’s
unlikely to happen immediately. Instead, we focus on building
relationships, sharing meaningful value, and establishing our
unique perspective on the world with our audience.
That builds a solid — purposefully inef�cient — foundation.
AoE is deliberately unpredictable as well. Think about the last
time you clicked on an ad — were you surprised by what
happened next? Probably not. Instead, you probably thought to
yourself:
“Here we go again…”

-- 4 of 22 --

Marketing has become increasingly formulaic. We know exactly
what to expect and that makes it really easy to tune out most
messaging.
(Is anyone ever surprised anymore when the fourth video in a
four-part launch series has an offer…or when sales emails start
pouring in moments after opting in for a lead magnet?)
We’re not suggesting that predictable methods don’t work — often
the problem is that they worked so well everyone copied the
methods.
However, once an audience has been saturated by the same
approach used over and over again (looking at you ‘free +
shipping’ book funnels), predictability becomes a problem.
AoE embraces unpredictability, in the spirit of Edward Luttwak’s
approach to strategy, to stand out from the crowd. This is effective
because it’s real — we’re not engineering something that looks
unpredictable (until it doesn’t).
Instead, we’re making decisions and taking actions that are
genuinely different. (We’ll explain more about those decisions
and actions, in detail, below.)
In order to understand mastery of emotions in the context of The
Art of Email, we need to take a step back and honestly assess the
dominant emotion that drives most marketing decision-making.
Fear.

-- 5 of 22 --

Why rush to get the sale as quickly as possible? Fear that the sale
won’t happen otherwise.
Why rely on ‘tried and true,’ predictable marketing methods that
everyone else is using too? Fear that anything else won’t work.
Why yell louder when everyone else is screaming for attention?
Fear that your audience won’t pay attention if you don’t.
The �rst step in mastering our emotions in the context of our
email marketing decisions is to recognize how much fear
in�uences those decisions. In that context, ‘conventional wisdom’
seems like the safe bet.
The Art of Email replaces fear with calm con�dence that is
informed by the reality of how the world actually works. Your
audience lives in a non-linear world that is not simple cause and
effect where A leads to B leads to C.
Instead, the results you’ll get from your email marketing are
properties that emerge from the interactions of the parts you’ll
create. It’s how those parts work together — not how they
function separately — that matters.
Once you see that reality (and you will if you do the work we
describe in the Art of Email), you can’t un-see it.
Calm con�dence is waiting for you on the other side of the work
you’ll do in AoE.

-- 6 of 22 --

Before we describe the components that will work together to
drive your email marketing strategy, let’s put strategy into a
larger, more complete (and useful) context.
The image below is from Beyond Entrepreneurship 2.0 by Jim
Collins & Bill Lazier.
Beyond Entrepreneurship 2.0, p. 91
Vision is the highest level.
This is where we determine what matters most to us as business
owners and creative professionals. Vision creates clarity, it reveals
and draws attention to “red lines” we’ll never cross.

-- 7 of 22 --

Vision is not just �owery words. It’s comprised of fundamental
beliefs which are demonstrated, without exception, through
behavior. Core values aren’t what you talk about; they’re what you
do when times get tough.
Collins and Lazier occasionally use terminology that’s more
appropriate for their corporate clients (for example, purpose and
mission).
A better way to think about those terms is by identifying your
overall business objectives. If you woke up tomorrow and your
business was perfectly successful, how would you know?
What would be happening?
That’s where you’ll �nd your objectives.
The central insight from this framework is that tactics �ow
from strategy, and strategy emerges in response to your
vision.
Strategy informs what tactics you use to get a job done.
Strategy is the long-term view of how you achieve a high-level
systemic objective. It’s the bridge between your goal (the function
of your business) and the tactics you use to execute your strategy.
Tactics are the only outward-facing activities that can be seen by
others (people watching what you’re doing; seeing your ads,
reading your website copy, your offers, your price points, etc.).

-- 8 of 22 --

The objectives of your business, and the strategy you embrace to
achieve those objectives, remain unseen (unless you choose to
reveal them).
Thankfully, our objectives and strategies as marketers and
creative professionals can be relatively simple (compared to a
nation-state or multinational company).
Examples are helpful, so for reference we’ll share elements of our
vision and our primary objective expressed through our TLB
brand.
Our vision can be summarized as a simple idea: marketing with
integrity.
Broadly speaking, that means we do what we say, and we say what
we do. There is no distance between the ideas we teach and who
we are as individuals.
If you ever meet us in person (and we hope you do), you’ll realize
immediately that we’re exactly the same as we appear in our
writing and other communications. We love what we do, study
our craft intensely, and constantly pressure-test, re�ne, and
expand our thinking.
Our business strategy is informed by several factors, including:
▪ WHO: we serve those who serve others (ideally: digital to
digital)
▪ HOW: we help creators bridge the gap between creating

-- 9 of 22 --

deep, meaningful work they’re proud of, and earning an
independent living (earning them the level of freedom they
desire).
▪ OBJECTIVE: create a happy customer factory (within a scale
that doesn’t undermine our love and desire for doing this
work).
Summarized, we serve �rst, for free, before money has ever
changed hands. Earning money is a downstream consequence of
serving �rst, with transparency, empathy, honesty, and integrity.
We treat everyone immediately as if they’re a customer. (See our
Strategic Upstream Decisions earlier in Module 1.)
The tactics that emerge as a result are by-products of our vision
and strategy. Those tactics change dynamically over time, but our
vision remains our North Star (and our strategy is always an
expression of that vision).
With all of that as background, let’s look at the component parts
of The Art of Email that will work together to create results for
you and your audience.
AoE: A 10,000′ View

-- 10 of 22 --

AoE Stack
The power of AoE is that it’s a holistic system that produces
emergent results. We’re going to deconstruct the parts that make
up the system. But it’s important to emphasize, once again, that
results emerge from the interaction of a system’s parts, not the sum
of the parts taken separately.
What’s more, once you’ve internalized the parts that make up an
AoE system, you get to assemble the parts in different and unique
ways that match the speci�c system you want to build (like
LEGO).
But before we get ahead of ourselves, here are the seven core parts
of AoE:
�. Relationship Building Series (RBS) — ENTRANCE (everyone!)
�. Value Newsletter (VNL) — ONGOING (everyone!)
�. Soap Opera Series (SOS) — TRIGGERED (based on interest)
�. Bridge Emails (soft and hard)
�. Story-Powered Promotion (SPP) — TRIGGERED (smaller
just-in-time segments; evergreen)
�. Product Launch Series (PLS) — TRIGGERED (smaller just-in-
time; time-bound)

-- 11 of 22 --

�. Customer Onboarding Series — TRIGGERED (after
purchase)
The �rst three parts make up the AoE’s Trust Engine (RBS + VNL
+ SOS). Parts �ve, six, and seven make up the Revenue Engine
(SPP + PLS + COS). Part 4 is the “bridge” that connects the two
engines of AoE, explicitly or implicitly.
The Trust Engine is where we establish rapport, authority, earn
trust and attention, and develop long-lasting relationships
through ongoing value creation. The Trust Engine touches
everyone. It’s the water within our email �shbowl.
The Revenue Engine is where we monetize attention. SPP for
evergreen offers, PLS for time-limited offers, and COS for
customers. The Revenue Engine never touches everyone all at
once.
It’s important to emphasize that AoE is not a collection of parts
arranged sequentially as a linear funnel.
The funnel metaphor doesn’t work, because by de�nition a funnel
has a top and a bottom and �ows in one direction — in the wide
top and out the narrow bottom. That’s a �awed way of thinking
about reality.
AoE’s seven parts are not linear. Each sub-system that makes up
AoE has a different texture (and sub-goal), that interconnect and
work together to produce desired results.

-- 12 of 22 --

AoE has a “�ow” — which then “triggers” downstream parts of the
system, some driven by subscriber behavior, others by just-in-time
interest.
The entry point for everyone is a Relationship Building Series
(RBS); either a single one for your whole business, or multiple
RBSs triggered by various entry points from your website.
Relationship Building Series (RBS)
This is the ENTRANCE for everyone. The role of a Relationship
Building Series is to quickly establish rapport, demonstrate
empathy and authority, and start on the path to earning trust and
attention.
Doing this well is how we start to develop a long-lasting
relationship with our audience as they enter our world (a
component of our ongoing worldbuilding efforts).
An RBS is an automated email sequence that has clearly de�ned
entry and end points. It could be seven emails over a week, ten
over two weeks, and in some cases longer.
There is no prescribed length. We don’t provide templates on
purpose. They’re restrictive at best, and hopelessly misleading at
worst.

-- 13 of 22 --

Once someone reaches the end, they’re “tagged” as completed and
then available to receive our newsletters (and/or can be “bridged”
into an SPP or PLS in some cases).
Value Newsletter (VNL)
Newsletters are sent manually as broadcasts (although they can
be automated within a sequence, but for most people, sticking to
broadcasts is the best option and simplest to implement).
The newsletter is a vehicle through which we provide ongoing
value.
A VNL has no endpoint. Its value creation effects are constant and
compounding, deepening and expanding our relationship with
each subscriber on our email list over time at scale.
It’s these three parts of the AoE system that enable the
opportunities for promotions to happen. They’re the foundation
that creates a deeper relationship (allowing everything that
follows to perform signi�cantly better).
Soap Opera Series
The role of a Soap Opera Series is to create opportunities to
deepen a relationship with a segment of subscribers who have

-- 14 of 22 --

self-selected.
An SOS can sell, but in the broad majority of cases, especially for
us in our business, an SOS doesn’t sell directly. It sets up an
opportunity to sell.
An SOS is triggered, and you can have as many of these as you
want.
Below is an example expression of a customer path.
A subscriber enters an RBS. A (Soft) Bridge transitions to a Story-
Powered promotion. The subscriber doesn’t buy. They’re added to
the VNL loop, and receive six newsletters over a period of time.
In the sixth VNL, they click a link to indicate their intent to
receive an SOS, framed as ‘Season 1’ of something valuable. The
SOS ends with a (Hard) Bridge, which they raise their hand to
receive.

-- 15 of 22 --

The promotion (PLS) ends with a sale, creating a paying customer
from this subscriber.
Their customer journey is unique. Other subscribers will take a
different path, a different ‘choose your own adventure’ journey
unique to them.
Some subscribers will turn into customer quickly (days), others
longer (weeks or months, maybe even years).
Story-Powered Promotion (SPP)
An SPP is a self-contained automated promotion around a single
big idea, theme, or marketing hook. For this reason, it’s very
focused and precise.
SPPs are a mechanism through which we monetize attention for
evergreen offers, and they’re triggered by individual behavior. An
SPP will never be sent to everyone at the same time.
For example: we have four core offers — the Art of Email, Sphere
of In�uence, The Traf�c Engine, and The Durable Business — but
they will never be relevant to everyone who enters our world at
the same time.
For this reason, it’s our job to create context for the value we send
through our Trust Engine, then invite, when the time is right,
interested people to self-select based on their needs and desires
through their behavior, driven by their current circumstances and

-- 16 of 22 --

environment (which is ever changing).
SPPs can be “stacked” around a single product — presenting
different angles and themes — and the logic of marketing
automation will automatically send an SPP within a stack to a
subscriber (never repeating a promotion).
SPPs are assets, and you get to create as many of them as you
want, then “load” them into your automation system. The system
will then manage who gets what, and when, based on their
unique needs demonstrated by their behavior.
We’ll cover SPPs in depth later, including different types of SPPs
(Direct vs. Showcase promotions).
Product Launch Series (PLS)
A PLS monetizes attention for time-limited offers. A PLS is more
akin to a typical product launch-style promotion (yet nothing like
PLF), delivered entirely through email.
Our methods are very different from what you’ve likely seen
before.
A PLS is sent as broadcasts, and are typically longer in duration
than an SPP. There’s a hard open and close.
Customer Onboarding Series (COS)

-- 17 of 22 --

A COS begins immediately after someone becomes a customer.
We include it as part of the Revenue Engine of the Art of Email,
but it’s really a hybrid that deepens trust as well.
Customer onboarding is an opportunity to reassure purchasers
they’ve made a good choice, draw attention to how best to engage
with whatever they’ve purchased, and engage with customers
over time to maintain a connection after a sale.
A Customer Onboarding Series can feel like a Soap Opera Series if
pulling your audience’s attention forward is a good �t for your
customers’ needs after a sale (for example, if you’re selling a
digital course).
—André & Shawn
A
Sekigahara (by André)
s far back as I can remember I’ve had a fascination with
strategy-based games. I remember when I was �ve or six — I
know this because the memory was of the house I grew up in, and
I was about seven when we left. My best friend, Jacque, and I
would spend endless hours playing Monopoly on his parents’ bed.
I loved Monopoly. Although it was a game of strategy, it was also a
game heavily in�uenced by luck (randomization). For this reason,
there was never a best player who would win more times than
not. At some point, I guess, this reality dawned on me, so
eventually I lost interest in playing Monopoly.

-- 18 of 22 --

Another favorite pre-Internet game was Risk: while luck was still
a factor (a characteristic of using dice for movements and battle),
good strategy would almost always win over poor strategy.
Then I found Magic: The Gathering (MtG), a deck-building card
game. MtG has no dice. You have a deck, you draw cards
(introducing uncertainty; a fundamental part of the game), you
execute a strategy based on the deck theme and archetype. I
collected thousands of cards. This was before everything went
online.
In January 2020, I listened to a brilliant interview (Ep. 85) between
Tyler Cowen (economist) and Reid Hoffman (co-founder of
LinkedIn), where Reid described his interest in playing Avalon Hill
board games:
COWEN: Avalon Hill board games. How good were you at them?
HOFFMAN: [laughs] I think you know the answer to this question,
which is, I don’t recall losing one.
COWEN: And which of your qualities are responsible for your being so
good at Avalon Hill board games?
HOFFMAN: I think what I did is, I learned at an early age what
counts as a strategy, how tactics relate to a strategy, and what
counts as a di�erential strategy when you are in a con�ict with
another person.
I was never as good as a number of people at chess and so forth. Those

-- 19 of 22 --

games always struck me as inaccurate to real world because they didn’t
have a gap between epistemology and metaphysics. They didn’t have
the uncertainty, the fog of war. They didn’t have randomness elements
to them, a bunch of things that are actually in fact very present in life.
Whereas Avalon Hill games — you had to have plans B. You had to
have — if the die roll went against you, what else would you do? What
was your fallback plan for what you’re doing? You had to have a model
of how the other person was thinking about it, and you had to be
thinking about what are the ways that you might see things coming
together in ways that the other person wouldn’t, within this fog of war
and fog of uncertainty.
COWEN: My favorite Avalon Hill game was Blitzkrieg. Have you ever
played it?
HOFFMAN: I don’t think I’ve played Blitzkrieg. I played Tactics II a lot,
but also a bunch of the other theater war ones, like North Africa and the
other ones.
In 2019, while researching new table top strategy-based board
games to buy, I came across Sekigahara: Uni�cation of Japan (I
have the 4th Printing).
The game seemed unique and interesting (block war game with
cards and no dice, removing randomization but keeping the ‘fog
of war’ of uncertainty high), but it wasn’t until I pulled on the
thread a bit more, that I discovered the billionaire designer and
creator.

-- 20 of 22 --

Matt Calkins (pictured above) is a billionaire who founded Appian
at age 26, a low-code platform as a service for building enterprise
software applications.
A history and board game a�cionado, Matt says: “I’ve loved board
games since I was too young to even count. I love the complexity
of them, how much data you can �t on a table, the competition
involved and the learning aspect. If you don’t win, you get instant
feedback in the result of the game so you can use that to learn and
change your approach for next time.”
Matt won ‘Wargame of the Year’ for a game he invented in 2011
called Sekigahara, and �nished 3rd overall in the 2012 World
Boardgaming Championships, held each year in Pennsylvania
with thousands of competitors.
“I feel like I should apologize,” Matt Calkins tells his opponent as they
shake hands. It’s mid-evening in the log-lined cafeteria at the Seven
Springs Mountain Resort, and Calkins, from a trailing position, has just
played a decisive move in the board game Sekigahara. His opponent

-- 21 of 22 --

shakes his head at the suggestion Calkins prevailed through luck. “You
sprung a trap on me,” he says.

-- 22 of 22 --
